skill.name: esimaccess-api
skill.description: Manage eSIM Access API workflows for balance checks, package discovery, eSIM ordering, status queries, usage monitoring, top-ups, cancellations, profile controls, webhooks, and refund or reissue support.
skill.summary: Operate and document eSIM Access API workflows from package lookup through provisioning and lifecycle management.
skill.author: esimaccess

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many shell execution and weak cryptography alerts, but review found those are mostly markdown backticks, example code fences, HMAC-SHA256 authentication examples, and formatted output. The confirmed risks are legitimate network calls to the eSIM Access API and a test script that can create orders, send SMS, suspend profiles, and cancel eSIMs when run with a real access code. No evidence found of prompt injection, hidden exfiltration, filesystem tampering, or malicious command execution.

medium_findings.title: State-changing API test script can affect real accounts
medium_findings.description: The included test script accepts a real access code, calls the production eSIM Access API, and can create an eSIM order, suspend and unsuspend a profile, send SMS, and cancel an eSIM. This matches the skill purpose, but users need explicit approval before running paid or account-changing operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly performs production API calls and state-changing operations after a user supplies credentials. The behavior is documented and aligned with the API skill, so this is elevated operational risk rather than confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Sensitive eSIM provisioning details are printed to console
medium_findings.description: The test script logs account and eSIM details including partial access code, ICCID, IMSI, QR code URL, short URL, and balance changes. These values can expose account inventory or provisioning links if terminal output is shared.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The logged fields are visible in the script and include identifiers and provisioning URLs. The script masks most of the access code, but it does not redact eSIM profile details.

medium_findings.title: Production network requests use user-supplied API credentials
medium_findings.description: The script builds HMAC-signed headers from the access code and sends POST requests to the production eSIM Access base URL. This is expected for the skill, but it requires careful credential handling and user consent for each action.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network and credential-signing flow is explicit and points to the declared API domain. No evidence found that credentials are sent to an unrelated endpoint.

low_findings.title: Static shell execution alerts are markdown false positives
low_findings.description: The many reported Ruby or shell backtick detections occur in markdown code formatting, inline endpoint names, example commands, and JavaScript template literals. No child_process, eval, shell spawn, or Ruby execution path was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax is visible as markdown fences, inline formatting, or template strings. A targeted search found no actual command execution APIs in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Static weak cryptography alerts are HMAC-SHA256 false positives
low_findings.description: The reported cryptography locations document and implement HMAC-SHA256 request signing. SHA-256 HMAC is not a weak digest pattern in this context, although the API design treats the access code as both identifier and signing key.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code and docs explicitly use sha256 with HMAC. The static label appears to trigger on cryptographic keywords rather than an actually weak algorithm such as MD5 or SHA-1.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs and webhook IPs are documented service endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs and IP addresses identify the eSIM Access API, developer console, examples, QR link examples, and webhook sender whitelist. These are expected documentation values and do not indicate hidden traffic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The locations are all visible documentation or the declared production API base URL. No evidence found of unrelated domains, paste sites, or covert callback endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Production POST wrapper for API calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The apiCall helper posts signed request bodies to the production eSIM Access API. This is required for the skill, but misuse can spend balance or change eSIM profile state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The wrapper is a real network execution path and all later tests use it. The endpoint is the declared API domain, so the concern is operational impact rather than stealth.

dangerous_patterns.title: Write endpoints include purchasing and profile management
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents write operations for order creation, top-up, cancellation, suspend, unsuspend, revoke, SMS, and webhook configuration. These operations can cost money or alter service state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly lists state-changing endpoints and describes their impact. These capabilities are legitimate but require approval safeguards.

content.value_statement: eSIM operations require careful handling of packages, orders, credentials, and profile state. This skill gives AI agents a structured reference for safe eSIM Access API workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains eSIM Access authentication with HMAC-SHA256 signed headers.
Documents balance checks, package listing, order creation, and order polling workflows.
Guides status, usage, top-up, cancellation, revoke, suspend, and unsuspend operations.
Provides webhook setup and query guidance for eSIM status notifications.
Includes a Node.js test script for end-to-end API validation with a user-supplied access code.
Clarifies API units for prices, bytes, duration, and status fields.
limitations: Requires a valid eSIM Access access code before live API calls can work.
Some operations can spend account balance or change active eSIM profiles.
The test script targets the production API and is not a sandbox harness.
The skill does not provide a hosted webhook receiver or credential vault.

use_cases.title: Validate eSIM API integration
use_cases.description: Run documented checks for authentication, package listing, ordering, polling, usage, and final balance verification.

use_cases.title: Support customer eSIM lifecycle requests
use_cases.description: Look up order state, ICCID details, usage, top-up eligibility, cancellation options, and profile status.

use_cases.title: Plan travel connectivity products
use_cases.description: Compare available packages by destination, data volume, duration, top-up support, and price units.

prompt_templates.title: Check account balance
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the eSIM Access API reference to explain how to check my account balance. Do not make a live request until I provide an access code and approval.

prompt_templates.title: Find packages for a destination
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the eSIM Access package lookup workflow for Japan. Include required fields, price conversion, and how to identify top-up support.

prompt_templates.title: Create and verify an order
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare an eSIM order workflow for package US_5_30. Ask for confirmation before any purchase, then describe how to poll for the ICCID.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a refund and reissue flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a safe refund and reissue plan for an unused eSIM. Verify eligibility, explain cancel versus revoke, and list every approval point.

output_examples.input: What packages are available for the United States?
output_examples.output: A concise package comparison with package name, package code, data volume, duration, converted USD price, and top-up eligibility.

output_examples.input: Can I cancel this unused eSIM and order a replacement?
output_examples.output: A step-by-step decision checklist covering status verification, cancellation eligibility, refund confirmation, replacement order approval, and ICCID polling.

output_examples.input: Why did my order not return an ICCID yet?
output_examples.output: An operational explanation that provisioning can take several seconds, with polling guidance and webhook fallback steps.

best_practices: Ask for explicit user approval before any order, top-up, SMS, suspend, revoke, cancel, or webhook change.
Redact access codes, ICCIDs, IMSIs, QR code URLs, and short URLs before sharing logs or summaries.
Start with read-only balance, package, and status calls before attempting state-changing workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not run the full production test script with a funded account unless purchases and cancellations are intended.
Do not treat ICCID and esimTranNo as interchangeable because some endpoints require a specific identifier.
Do not configure webhook URLs without verifying ownership and HTTPS delivery.

faq.question: Does this skill make live API calls by itself?
faq.answer: The skill is documentation and a test script. Live calls happen only when an agent or user runs code with valid credentials.

faq.question: Can this skill spend money?
faq.answer: Yes. Order and top-up operations can spend account balance when executed against the production API.

faq.question: What credential does the API require?
faq.answer: The API uses an access code in signed request headers. The documentation shows HMAC-SHA256 signing.

faq.question: Can I use it only for read-only tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. Balance, package listing, order status, eSIM status, data usage, and webhook query operations are read-oriented.

faq.question: Why are there security warnings?
faq.answer: The package contains production network calls and state-changing API workflows. Static shell and weak-crypto alerts were reviewed as false positives.

faq.question: Does it include webhook server code?
faq.answer: No. It documents webhook configuration and notification types, but it does not host or implement a receiver.
